CHAPTERONE The lndifferent Generation lndijference to God Breeds Indijference to Everything W hat's that noise? - lt's an aeroplane, Geronda. - Close the window, we don't want it coming in! The world has gone so mad even that could happen! ... Everything's falling apart, family, education, public services ... People don't care about anything! They are empty... - Whose fault is it that we have come to this? - I'm speaking in general terms; I just want to show you how indifferent people are. Go to any school and you '11 see. For example, if open windows are banging in the wind, it's doubtful that even one child will care enough to shut them so they don't break. Sorne will gape, sorne will just sit back and watch them rattle, while others will merely pass by as if nothing is happening. Indifference!
An army officer who was in charge ofthe oil depot used to tell me, 'Tm having a hard time finding a soldier to place on guard duty at the depot, one who will be responsible

34 THE RESPOI\'SIBILITY OF LOVE enough to keep others from starting a fire or be sure that he himselfwon 't throw a cigarette." There is a half-hearted spirit, no manliness at all. We have completely gone bad! I wonder how God tolerates us! There was so much dignity in the old days! There was philotimo! During the war in 1940, at the frontier, the Italians would sometimes have an encounter with the Greek guards and would visit their guard post. And to see the philotimo ofthe Greeks! Once when the Italians went to a Greek guard post, the Greeks offered them sorne coffee. One of the Greek officers took a handful of money out of his pocket, fifty and hundred drachma notes - and this money was worth something back then - and he set them on fire in order to boil the coffee. He wanted to show the Italians that Greece was a rich country! The Italians were impressed. See the spirit of sacrifice!

Nowadays we have been permeated by a spirit that one finds in communist countries. In Russia, even though they had an abundant crop this year, 1 there will be great hunger among the people. They did not harvest the grain on time; they went to harvest in the autumn. Is harvesting ever done in autumn? But if the crop is not their own, how can they ache enough for it to go and harvest? Their life is a drudgery. They don 't have the drive to go and create something, because for so many years they were not free to create. There it goes, with an idle spirit, with this indifference, the whole country is sinking.ls it raining and the wheat is spread out? They don't care. ls it time to leave work? They leave work. The rain washes the harvested wheat away. The next day they will go to work
1. This was stated in 1990.

The lndifferent Generation 35 on time to gather what is left of it! But when the wheat is your own and it's spread out on the threshing floor, if it should rain, would you ever leave it to be lost? No, you lose your sleep in order to save it. And, even though you're so very tired, you feel joy, you feel delight. lndifference towards God leads to indifference towards everything else; it leads to disintegration. Faith in God is so very important. When man worships God then he !oves his parents too, and his home, his relatives, his work, his village, his whole region, his State, his fatherland. One who does not !ove God or his family isn't capable ofloving anything; and, of course, he doesn 't lo ve his country either, which after all is but one big family. What l'm trying to say is that everything stems from faith in God. When a person does not believe in God, he doesn't care about his family, his village, or his country either.

And all of these values are now falling apart, and this is why there is a spirit of idleness and indifference. A poli ce officer wrote tome, "1 couldn't come because 1 hada lot of work to do. There's only two of us covering an area that is supposed to be covered by eight." You see what's happening? lnstead of assigning two more policemen, they're leaving only two!

Fortunately, there are exceptions. Once a father carne tome and said, "Pray for my son Angel o because they're going to kili him." 1 had known his son, who was serving in the military at the time, since childhood. "Why, what's wrong?" 1 asked him. He said, "He found the others playing cards when they should have been on duty. He reprimanded them; they didn't listen. Then he went to report them and one ofthem threatened to kili him." "Look," 1 said, "they're not going to kili him, so don't worry about

36 THE RESPO"'SIBILITY OF LOVE that. But 1 will pray for Angel o so they do not court-martial him for not playing cards with the others!" lleamed something else and said, "Glory be to God, there are still sorne Greeks who care about their country." An air force pilot had attempted to overtake sorne Turkish planes which had violated Greek air space, to take pictures in order to prove it. The others from the ground radio communications told him, "Let them go, don'! bother." But he insisted and kept trying to overtake them. In the meantime, the Turkish pilot with the bigger aeroplane was flying faster and lowered bis altitude, leading the poor Greek pilot to fall into the sea!
And there are others who simply fly around in their aeroplane! What a difference!

One has to grasp the real meaning, to have a need to do what is good and right, otherwise a11 is idleness and indifference. You can't possibly force someone to go fight in a war! He '11 be looking for ways to run rather than fight. But once he understands what harm the enemy can do, then he '11 volunteer to go.

People Today Are So Self~Centred In the o id days in Pharasa, my homeland, people used to say, "If you have sorne work that needs doing, don'! leave it for tomorrow. Ifyou have sorne good food, save it for tomorrow when a visitor might come." Nowadays people think, "Let's leave work for tomorrow, when someone might come to help us, and let's eat the good food tonight!" Today, most people are self-centred. They think only of themselves. Let's say it starts raining very heavily. You'11 see that most ofyou wi11 start thinking of The Indifferent Generation 37 whether you ha ve laundry hanging on the clothesline and wi11 hurry out to pick it up. This is not a bad thing, but our thoughts do not go any further. Even if they get wet, the clothes wi11 dry again. But what about !hose people threshing their harvest? Do you feel for them, offer them a prayer? Or, when lightning strikes; it is doubtful if a handful of people wi11 think of the farmers or those who have greenhouses.

People do not think of others, they don't go beyond themselves; their thoughts constantly revolve around themselves. But when they revolve around themselves, they have themselves at the centre, not Christ. They function outside the axis which is Christ. In order to be able to think of others, the mind mus! first be with Christ. Then one can also consider the neighbour, the animals and a11 of nature. His radio station is working and, as soon as a message is received, he runs to help. If our mind is not on Christ, the heart is not working, and, as such, we !ove neither Christ nor our fe11ow human beings, not to mention nature, the animals, the trees, the plants.

By living this way, you will never reach the point of having communication with the animals, the birds! If a bird were to fa11 from the roof, you would feed it. But if it does not fa11 from the roof, you do not think about feeding it. When I loo k at the birds, 1 say to myself, "The poor birds need to be fed," and I throw sorne crumbs, I leave out sorne water. I see diseased branches on the trees and I immediately think of cutting them off, so the res! of the tree doesn 't get infected. Even when a door or a window is banging, that's where my thought goes. I wi11 forget myself, if I should be needing something at that moment, and will tend to the door or the window, so that

38 THE RESPONSIBILITV OF LOVE they will not be damaged. I might think of myself as an afterthought. And if someone thinks and cares about the other creatures, how much even more must he think and care about their Creator! If man does not live like this, how will he ever communicate with God?
Even when you simply go out, take a look around.

Someone may have carelessly or maliciously - and l do hope no one would do that - thrown something to cause a fire. So take a look around. This, too, is a spiritual act, for even a glance can bear !ove. When l come out of my Kalyvi, T take a look downwards and l take a look up towards the roof to see if something is buming. Unless, of course, one has such faith that if something does catch fire, prayer alone will put out the fire. Otherwise, one must take action. Also, if 1 hear an explosion, Tpay close attention to see what it is. Ts it a gun? Are they practi~ ing? Is it a blasting-charge? My mind runs immediately to such concems so that 1 may direct m y prayer with the kombosch(!ini. One who disregards himself, out of !ove for others, will have the great care of God, and all people wi ll care for him.

But today's generation is the generation of indifference! Most people are only for the parades! lfsomething were to happen, don 't even try telling them to defend themselves! And nowadays they don't like parades either! In the past, at least, people would go to the parades, listen to a marching tune, and something would stir inside them! Today, we Greeks are idle and lack discipline. Of course, other nations are even worse, without any ideals.

You see, the Greeks may have a whole lot of faults and blemishes in their character, but they have one gift from God: philotimo and leventia (bravery). They celebrate The ln<lifferent Generation 39 everything. Other nations do not even have these words in their vocabulary.
We Have a Responsibility 2 An atheist to the bone had come to my Kalyvi once.

After going on and on, he final! y told me, "Yo u know, Tam an iconoclast." He believed in nothing and yet he claimed to be an iconoclast. So 1 told him, "You godless fellow, how can you talk to me about being an iconoclast, when you believe in nothing? At the time of the Tconoclastic Controversy, 3 sorne Christians fell into error through excessive zeal and went to the other extreme, but then the Church resolved the issue; it was never a matter of not believing in God." He then went on to support the present situation. And then 1 really became angry with him. "How can the present situation be acceptable to you?" I told him. "Judges afraid to judge; indictments against criminals withdrawn because someone or another threatens them. Who is running the country? Are you comfortable with this situation? lf you support such people, you're a criminal yourself. Why did you cometo me? Get out ofhere!" And 1 sent him away. - Geronda, aren 't yo u afraid to speak like that? - What is there to be afraid of? 1 have already dug my grave. lf I had not dug my own grave, then 1 would

' ¡iil 40 THE RESPONSIBILITY OF LOVE be concemed about the person who would tire himself digging it. As it is now, someone only has to throw in a few shovelfuls of soil... 1 have in mind another atheist, a blasphemer, who has said the most blasphemous things about Christ and Panaghia, one who is permitted to appear on television.

Even the Church does not take a stand to excommunicate sorne of these people. Such people should be excommunicated, but the Church seems reluctant todo so! - Geronda, what would be accomplished with an excommunication when these people do not believe in anything anyway? - 1t will at least show that the Church has taken a stand on the matter. - Geronda, is the silence of the Church an indication of approval? -Yes. Someone wrote sorne blasphemous things about Panaghia and no one spoke up. Then 1 told someone, "Did you see what so-and-so has written?" And he told me, "Well, what can you do with those people? You'll get soiled ifyou try to deal with them." They're afraid to speak up. - What did he have to fear, Geronda? - That people might write something about him and ridicule him in the press. And so he tolerates blasphemous things about Panaghia! We want others to pull the chestnuts out of the tire so that we can have our peace of mind. This indicates a lack of !ove. Then man begins to act out of self-interest. This is why we see an all-toofamiliar spirit today: "Get to know so-and-so because he will then speak well of you. Be on good terms with soand-so and he will not speak badly of yo u, and so forth. p The_ll]dJffercnt Generation 41 After all, we must not be taken for fools, we must not become victims." Another person remains indifferent and does not speak up. "[do not speak out," he says, "so that I won't be written up in the newspapers." In other words, most people are completely indifferent. For a long time no one would write anything, but now sorne little effort is being made. Years ago 1 had scolded someone on the Holy Mountain. "You are over-patriotic!" he retorted.

Not too long ago he carne back to find me and say "They have destroyed everything, family, education ... " And 1, in tum, told him, "You are over-patriotic!" This whole situation has led to one bad thing and one good thing. The bad thing is that indifference has overtaken so many people, people who used to have something in them, but who now just say, "Who am 1 to sol ve the situation?" The good thing is the many people who are beginning to be troubled and to change for the better.

Sorne even cometo me and seek to justify a bad thing they did previously because they are now troubled by it. - Geronda, are we then always to confess our faith? - Discemment is needed. There are times when we should not speak out, and other times when we should confess our faith with courage, otherwise we will bear a responsibility for not speaking out. During these difficult years each one of us must do whatever is humanly possible; whatever is not humanly possible, leave it to God. This way we will have a clear conscience that we did what we could. If we do not resist these evil things, our ancestors will rise up from their graves against us.
They endured so many hardships for the homeland and what are we doing for it? Greece, Orthodox Christianity, with her Sacred Tradition, her Saints and heroes, is under

42 THE RESPONSIBILITY OF LOVE attack by the Greeks themselves, and we do not speak out! It is a terrible thing! 1 told someone, "Why don 't you speak out against what so-and-so is saying?" And he replied, "What can one say; that person is totally depraved!" "Jfhe is totally depraved, all the more reason to speak out and strike him down." They do nothing, they let him go on with his depravity. I actually spat at one of the politicians in disdain. "Say, at least, that you do not agree. Be forthright. Your attitude is that as long as you're all right, everything else can be ruined." lf Christians do not confess their faith, if they do not react, such people will do even worse things. But ifthey react, then they'll think twice about it. But 1 suppose many Christians nowadays are not made for battles. The early Christians were tough nuts; they transformed the world. And during the Byzantine period, if even one lcon was removed from the Churches, the people rose up in protest. Here Christ was crucified so that we may be resurrected, and we remain indifferent! lf the Church does not speak up so as to avoid a conflict with the State, if the metropolitans do not speak up in order to be on good terms with everybody - and especially with those who help them with the church foundations, ifthe monks of the Holy Mountain do not speak up for fear oflosing their subsidies,' who is going to speak up? 1 told one of the abbots, "lf they threaten to cut off your subsidy, tell them, 'We will then no longer provide hospitality to the pilgrims,' so they will think twice about that." The

The lndifferent Generation 43 professors of theology al so do not speak out against these abuses. They reason: "We are civil servants; we willlose our salary and how are we then to live?" In the meantime, the monasteries have been caught up with the pensions.

Why do you suppose that 1 do not want to receive even the small pension from the farmers' insurance fund (O.G.A.)? It is not honourable for a monk to be insured as a farmer. To be insured as an indigent, yes, l can see that; it honours the monk. But to be insured as a farmerwhy? A monk has disclaimed fat pensions, abandoned the world and come to the monastery, to receive a pension?

We betray Christ for a mere pension! - Geronda, is it acceptable for a nun, who may have worked as a teacher for sorne years, to receive her rightful pension? - Well, that's all right. But let me tell you, by giving this pension where it is needed, the nun will receive a good "pension" from Christ. 1 See What Awaits U1· and 1 A m Troubled The years pass, and what difficult years they are! Our troubles are not over. The cauldron is boiling. lf one is not strengthened spiritually, how can one face a difficult situation? God has not created human beings who are good for nothing. We must cultivate our phi!otimo. Indeed, if a great shake-up were to come upon us, God forbid, how many would remain standing?

Before the 1940 war, in Konitsa, where 1 had the carpentry shop near the market place, the villagers used to bring corn and wheat and so on. When these villagers would bring their corn to sell at the market, sorne wealthy

44 THE RESPONSIBILITY OF LOVE people- who were not really wealthy, but they did eam a small interest on their deposited money- would kick the sack and disinterestedly ask how much it cost. But when war carne and they had to sell everything they owned in order to survive, these same people would come to the market and when the villager said "Good moming", they would ask, "Have you any com to sell?" This is why you should thank God for all things. ~ten your belts a little and become frugal,lset!_what awaits us and 1 amJl'ltúbled. Do not allow yourselves to g~t slack. Have you any idea what Christians are going through in other places? 5 In Russia they are still in labour camps. What hardships they have to endure! There are no books for spiritual edification. And what about poor Albania and the great misfortune there? They don't even have anything to eat. Churches and Monasteries have all been shut down. People even had to change their names so they don 't sound Christian. Even in America, the Orthodox Christians are few and scattered in different places. Do you know what they're going through? Without an Orthodox community, they have to take the train miles and miles away just to go to Church. Sorne cometo the Holy Mountain to seek spiritual guidance. Tt is great ingratitude on our part to have such an enervate spirit prevailing in Greece.

"I cannot agree with ' this, kili me, throw me into prison," they would say, so

It would be different if they were going through sorne hardships, a war, or difficult years. But now, it's as if nothing is going on. How should one say this? It's like someone arriving from Australia by aeroplane to Greece in the springtime and then departing for Australia in autumn, only to arrive there in springtime again. They go from spring to spring, with no winter in sight, no sense of cold and heavy weather. - Geronda, how can we help a person who is indifferent? - We need to instil in him the good concem, the good restlessness, perturb him so that he' 11 want todo something to help himself. Tt cannot be done by force. A person must be thirsty befare you can give him water. Try giving food to someone who isn 't hungry; he will throw u p. When the other person does not want something, l cannot deprive him ofhis freedom, his free will. lgnorance Js Not Justifiable - Geronda, is it possible that sorne people are indifferent because they are simply ignoran! and uninformed? - What ignorance? Let me tell you about a case of ignorance. A literature teacher from Chalkidiki did not know what the Holy Mountain is. A German teacher spoke to him about the Holy Mountain and they carne together for a visit. The German man even knew how many monasteries there are on the Holy Mountain. And even though a Protestan!, he also knew about the sacred Relics and where each one was. Now, is such ignorance

46 THE RESPONSIBILITY OF LOVE justifiable among our people? Another person from America advised someone from Chalkidiki again to cometo me for help with a problem he was having. From America, mind you! Let me tell you something else.

Someone carne to my Kalyvi from Florina. I asked him, :_¿)- "Are you from within Florina?" And he said, "Yes, from ~ ' min the city." So I told him, "YQu ha~.e a very good r\V'3 lfo+' Metropohtanthere.'_' Then he asked me, What~ tearn does he play w1th?" He thought that I was talkmg about a football player. He was so immersed in the game of football that he didn 't even know the local bishop - at least Augustine Kantiotes is a well-known Bishop. Such ignorance is not justifiable.
No, ignorance cannot be justified in the world today.

What is missing is philotimo, the good disposition. He who has the good disposition ofcoming to know Christwilkome to know Him. He will makearum andcome to meet Him. And even if there is not a theologian or a monk to teach him the word of God, as long as he has good disposition he will make the tum to Christ either from an encounter with a snake, or a wild beast, or from a lightning, from a storm, or sorne other event in his life.
God will provide the means.

A young anarchist had gone to Germany. There he was confined to a reformatory because he had gotten involved with drugs. He had no help from anyone. In the reformatory someone gave him a N ew Testament. He read it and immediately changed. He said, "I will go to Greece, that's where Orthodoxy is." He retumed to his village, and his relatives were intent on finding him a wife. He got married and had a child. He continued to read the New Testament, go to Church, observe the fasts and the feasts. - The Indiffcrcnt Generation 47 The others who saw him live like that said, "He must have gone crazy to be reading the New Testament!" After a while his wife abandoned him and took the child with her. When his wife left him, he left everything he had in the village, fields, tractor, and so on and went to live asan ascetic in a cave. A Spiritual Father however advised him to go find his wife first, talk with her and then decide what to do. So he set out for Thessaloniki to find his wife and child. He believed, since the Spiritual Father so advised him, that Christ would reveal her. Christ did not reveal his wife in Thessaloniki. In the meantime, he found sorne Germans and instructed them in the faith. One of them was baptized. The Germans paid the fare for the young manto go to Athens to seek his wife there. But he did not find her there either. Again, someone helped him go to Crete. He started working there and went to see a Spi ritual Father. When the Spiritual Father heard his problem, he told him, "Is your wife and child like this, like that; there is a woman working here who arrived recently." And he described the woman to him. "She must be the one," the young man said. The Father informed her andas soon as she looked upon her husband, she exclaimed, "How did you find me? Are you a sorcerer? Did you find me with the help of sorcery?" S he tumed away and left him again before they could even begin to talk. He lost her for a second time.

He heard about me and carne to my Kalyvi. He knocked once and waited. He stood to the side doing prostrations until I would open the door. He was wearing old and wom clothing. He told me the whole story. 1 had sorne dry figs and offered them to him. "Do you want sorne figs?" 1 asked him. "I have no teeth," he replied. "1 don 't have

48 THE RESPONSIBILITY OF LOVE: any either," I told him. "Are you in pain?" he asked me and added, "I am in pain, and out of pain comes the joy of Christ." I asked him again, "Do you want a sweater?" "I have two," he said, "and if the weather warms up I will give one away to someone." Then I told him, "Until you clear up the situation with your wife, you must take care of your health because you have a responsibility to your child too." Such self-denial, such faith that young man had! He wasn't yet twenty-seven years old. Imagine if he had come to know the monastic life! He had total ignorance, but he also had a good disposition and God helped him to make profound evangelical progress.

This is why I say that ignorance cannot be justified in any way today. Only if someone is mentally deficient ora small child can he be j ustified for being ignoran t. But then again, even little children today are so bright! If one really wants to, there are many opportunities to leam the truth. • CHAPTERTWO The Presence of a Christian Is a Confession of Faith (j eronda, do the various movements, demonstrations and protests carried out by Christians have any positive results? - The presence of Christians is now a confession of faith. Someone may be of more help through prayer, but his silence will be exploited by others who'll say, "So and so did not protest, therefore he is on our side, he agrees with us." If sorne ofus do not begin to strike back at evil, to reproach those who scandalize the faithful, greater harm will be done. The faithful will be somewhat encouraged, and at the same time those who are attacking the Church will be somewhat hindered. The Church is not their boat to go sailing with; it is the ark of Christ.

And those who attack the Church are censurable. They are only interested in having big salaries, expensive cars, and entertainment... And then they make laws for civil marriagej, they_l~galize abortions ... As to what God wlll 1fcl,That is anOther inatter. ---~ And with the blasphemous movies being produced these days, they wiirinú rí31culeLhrist. Essentially, they

50 THE RESPONSIBILITY OF LOVE are saying "That's who Christ was, but now will come the real Messiah." Then they go on to present their own "Messiah". This is what they seem to be aiming a t. - People, Geronda, believe these things and are harmed! - Those already harmed are harmed all the more. People believe these things because they want to justify the unjustifiable and to ease their conscience. With all this blasphemy they are seeking to justify moral and spiritual disorders. They have overdone it. They filed a lawsuit against the movie, The Last Temptation of Christ, 1 as being blasphemous to the Christian religion, and the public prosecutors said, "Oh, it is nothing." Such blasphemies had not been heard before! For us the protest against that blasphemous movie was a confession of faith. Of course, with all these blasphemous things there also comes a good thing: the wheat is separated from the chaff, people are passed through the sieve. - Geronda, are there issues which we as individuals or as groups should defend, and other issues which we should not? When you, for example, were called a heretic, you responded, bl,IKV!t1i her criticisms you kept silent. - This is the. ol:lsensus f the Holy Fathers, 2 and not my own opiníon. ny o er accusation helps me in the

The Prcsence of a Christian 51 spiritual life, while the accusation of being a heretic separates me from Christ.

People Are Being Lulled to S!eep - Geronda, with everything that is going on in the world today, how can people get sorne help? - The one who really desires to be helped can indeed be helped through insignificant things. For example, a vigil oil lamp is seen moving back and forth, or the person himself is shaken by an earthquake and he recovers from his indifference. But !hose who do not believe in the first place, become worse, when they hear that a war will break out or sorne catastrophe will take place. "Let's have a good time now," they reason, "life's too short." So they pull out all the stops. In the past even the indifferent ones would recover and change their way of Ji fe when a war was about to break out. Now such people are very few. In the past our nation Ji ved spiritually, and God would bless it, and our Saints helped us overcome our enemies, who were always more numerous than us.
Today we say that we are Orthodox Christians, but unfortunately we often only have the name Orthodox but not the orthodox way oflife.

Once I asked a Spiritual Father who was active in society and had many spiritual children, "What do you know about a blasphemous movie?" "I know nothing about it," he told me. He knew nothing about it and yet was guiding so many people in a big city. People are being lulled to sleep. They want people in the dark, carefree and having a good time. One must not dare to speak about an impending war, or that we mus! prepare

52 TIIF: RESPONSIBILITY OF LOVE ourselves for the Second Coming. God forbid that we should cause-1he peopleto worry over such things! lt is like sorne old ladies who say, "Don't talk about death; talk only of joyful things and baptisms ... " As if death is not waiting for them too. So they have a false sense of joy. On the other hand, if they were to think about the aged man down the street who died yesterday, or the other one who has come to the end of his life and will soon die, or that a memorial service is being held the da y after tomorrow for someone much younger than them, then they might be inclined to think about death and say, "! must go to confession, prepare myself spiritually, for Christ may call me soon to the other life." Otherwise, death in time will take them unprepared.

Sorne others again, out of supposed "kindness", give the following advice: "Don't tell the heretics that they are in error, to show them !ove." So, everything is levelled. If these people had Ji ved during the early years of Christianity, we wouldn 't have hada single Saint. In those .~ears Christians were told, "Just throw sorne incense in the fire for the emperor and do not deny Christ." And they would not accept this. "Just pretend you're throwing incense." Again, they refused. "Don't talk about Christ and go quietly to sorne other area." Again, they did not accept any compromise. The Benevolent God provides His divine enlightenment to those who ha ve good intentions. A judge once related a case which he had encountered personally. A monk was sent by his monastery with five-hundred gold coins to buy a field. He went to ask a real estate broker, who said to him, "Leave the gold coins with me so you don't have to carry them." So he left the money with the broker. "What a nice man," the monk thought innocently in his heart, "he relieved me of that burden." When the monk retumed, the broker not only did not give him back the coins but even claimed that he had given the monk eight million drachmas! The poor monk thought how he would retum to the monastery. Not only had he given the five-hundred coins and received nothing in retum but the broker was demanding an additional eight mi Ilion drachmas. The matter ended up in court.
